body_txt: The primary reason for the incest taboo across most human cultures and even among animals is to minimize the spread of detrimental recessive genes. However, despite the risks of incest, some people choose to do it anyway, and some of those people end up having children. While there is no guarantee that an inbred child will possess birth defects, statistically speaking, they are more likely to, especially if it occurs over multiple generations. In addition, entire communities can be plagued with genetic disorders because of the population only breeding within itself. There are multiple ways this trope can appear in fiction. Sometimes a character's mental and/or physical ailments might be explained as them being inbred. Some communities in fiction that are physically or socially isolated might be subject to widespread genetic disorders because the population is incredibly inbred. In royal and noble families, it's not unknown for a Royally Screwed Up family to be that way because of Royal Inbreeding . Speculative fiction might have Fictional Disabilities be caused by inbreeding. Like all scientific concepts, fiction has a habit of exaggerating how quickly this can occur. Inbreeding cannot create defects out of nothing; the issue is that most hereditary health defects are passed by recessive genes, so if they get inherited, the lack of genetic diversity means they can't be counteracted. Thus whilst it is possible, it's extremely unlikely for offspring to develop multiple or severe defects from a single case of incest, which usually requires multiple generations, but in fiction it does regularly happen as shown by several examples. A Sister Trope to, and may frequently coexist with, Inbred and Evil . Compare/contrast Hybrid Power , in which hybrid DNA makes someone more powerful. While this trope does certainly happen in Real Life , to avoid it taking up the page, No Recent Examples, Please! .
